posts.text: New technology moves the baseline. The generation that saw its arrival is thrown through a loop, but the generation that grows up with it is going to characterize a new distribution over capabilities, and people who care about unique expression will continue to find ways to distinguish their voice.

reply_context.text: The way I tend to frame it is that it's in "opposition to craftsmanship" in thee exact same way as owning a 3D printer or CNC machine. For some people it's just a shortcut, but for others (esp. who already had high in-domain skill before the new toy) it opens doors to new forms of expression.

posts.text: PS: this rule of thumb has been making the rounds since at least 1988. Aged like wine.

reply_context.text: The rule of thumb is "If it's easy for humans, it might be deceptively hard for AI. If it's hard for humans, it might be deceptively easy for AI." Simple arithmetic is the kind of thing you'd think it ought to be good at, but generative brains are layers of approximations.

reply_context.text: Most of the things I use AI for are things I can do myself, but I can leverage AI to achieve those outcomes faster and with less effort. For example, I've used LLMs to code in unfamiliar languages and frameworks, and to build momentum on projects that I was struggling to start (ADHD).

reply_context.text: AI is "amazing" if you have a particular personal capability gap that you can use AI to bridge. Wheelchairs are amazing for people with mobility problems. If you're ambulatory it's an inconvenience, even if it does take the weight off your legs. That wasn't something you were struggling with.
